speaker: interviewer
text: G-E-R-S-H-K-O-W-I-T-Z. This is roll four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Saul, we were talking briefly at the end of the other tape about the fires that you told me about. I just wanted to make sure we didn't leave that out.

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The fires. The 40 fires.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the, the-- well, that was something to me too. I was young at that time. And they made us come down and put out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- well, the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Medvedev's group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medvedev's group. They had-- they had to get a plane down in middle of the night. And the plane had to-- had to know where to come in. So the-- we had to make fires on both side of the runway, which it was a field. And he came down and was, was right in the, in the, in the middle of the Germans. And, you know, we were moving around like we owned the country at that time. And the plane did come down and took whatever-- gave whatever he had to. He took whatever he had to. And he went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian plane.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Propeller. No, no jet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you ever do harassing of the Germans? Did you ever do any sabotage of the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We went-- you don't go alone. You go a few people together, or you put a mine under, under a train, under the tracks, or-- we used to do-- after we came out by ourselves and the people of the Ukraine in which they were bad, we used to burn a house down. We used to slaughter some of the cows, which we can use for food by ourselves. We did whatever we can, and we ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell me? But was it you who told me about burying a cow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not burying a cow. We-- at that time, my sister-in-law Reina's stepfather was with us. And he was a [NON-ENGLISH]. What is a [NON-ENGLISH] in-- you know, a guy what drives a horse and wagon. What is a [NON-ENGLISH], yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So we caught a-- we needed some food. So we caught a cow. And he took a big log and he knocked the cow right in the head. And the cow fell down. And then we slaughtered the cow. A lot of us used the blood which came out. And we cooked it on the fire because we use it for salt. We ate it like liver. When you cook blood, it becomes hard, and you eat like liver. And then we used it--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the salt.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the salt, because there was no salt. And we used the meat. Some of them we used right away. The next, you know, you, you bury it in the skin of the cow. You bury it in the-- in the ground. And if it's still good after a few weeks, you eat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You cook it and eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK, now we're going to jump right back into Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Linz. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and, and after the-- by April, you were in-- you were back up near your old area in-- not Grodno. Radno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rovno. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rovno. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: April 12 of '45, you were up near Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you found out about Roosevelt's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was Rovno. This was-- this was-- I was still in Berezne. 1945, I was still in Berezne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The end of April '45, by the end of April, the European war was over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Over. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go then, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there. And from there we went to Austria-- I mean, to, to Czechoslovakia, Pilsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Pilsen we went to, to Linz, Austria. And that's where we stayed from 1945 till 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for those four years? You were a young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a young man. I was finding out about women. I was finding out to make a living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You still want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were a bad boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bad boy. We used to take out Germans from a nightclub and beat them a little bit and dislodge them a little bit and then go our own way. We weren't afraid of anything. Two of them were very, very strong guys. Another few were very, very bad guys. And we survived that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no families. They had nothing left.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I had a brother at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And another brother was alive in, in, in, in Russia at that time. He still didn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You told me that things were so bad at the beginning. At the end of the war in some of these camps, that these-- there were brothers with sisters and, and, and tremendous mixing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mixing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was confusion and there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of-- I saw-- I saw a sister and a brother living together. And I saw a mother and a daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Share a man. And at that time, it was such a trauma. And it was already so many months after the war. But still, in, in, inside your brain was bad. And whatever you could get of life while you still, you, you took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever take time, a few of you, to sit after the war and ponder and wonder and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm still pondering and wondering. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I had my own family, I didn't talk much about it. I told them a story. We never-- I never talked about it in the community where I used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get to that nice community, let's come to 1949. You spent three and a half, almost four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a lot of friends. Had a lot of friends. We used to go-- afterwards we used to go to nice resorts. Like, I spent a few weeks, nice few weeks, in Bad Gastein, which it's a resort where Hitler used to come down for the bets. You know, they had the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they made it DP camps for the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had money. I used to-- I used to make money on the black market. I had nice clothes at that time. I had one suit when I came to this country, a nice one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to this country, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they sent papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your, your family, your father's family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the papers said that, if I don't get a job, they're going to be responsible for me, and they're going to be responsible for my housing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did those papers reach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before. The UNRRA advertised the names of the survivors and they find out. And they send us papers through the UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: To that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: To that camp. And the UNRRA, the-- I think the UNRRA or the-- paid for us. They didn't pay our passage. The Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Joint.

speaker: interviewee
text: Joint paid passage for me to come. But when I came there, my family was in Boston. I came straight to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the sake of history, let's say this was the Joint Distribution Committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so you left Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Austria through Bremerhaven.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what route? Bremerhaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bremerhaven. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Remember the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boat was the It was a, a boat which they used to bring back wounded people and--

speaker: interviewer
text: American boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: American, yeah. American soldiers, troops. And I went with my brother. My-- Julius, who was married at that time, and he had a baby-- they took him out from-- six months before us. And single people were later. And that's how I came in 1949, August.

speaker: interviewer
text: August of '49, you landed in Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: I landed in Boston and my--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came down the gangplank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my two cousins was there, and my brother was there with his wife. And they took me to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the emotions or the feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember very, very well. My aunt, who was a very old, pious woman, saw me and she thought that I am God himself who came down. And that night, I remember they took me out to Revere Beach for supper. And they bought me a pizza, which is the first time I had pizza in my life. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, I was 24 years old. I, I came on a Sunday. My brother already had an apartment in Chelsea. He didn't live with the aunt any more. And I stayed in a room with my brother. And the, the landlord of my brother's apartment took me to a place in Chelsea, woodworking shop, which they were making cabinets and things. And he got me the job as sweeper of the floor. And there was a Polish-- Christian Polish guy who worked there. He had a bench. He's the one who I was speaking to Polish, and he tell the boss in English whatever. Because at that time, I only spoke Polish and Jewish. And after six months, I did just as good or maybe better than the Polish guy and other guys there. So I asked-- I went in and I told the Polish, tell me how to say it. So I went in, and I said, I want a raise. And the, the guy said, I can't give you a raise. And I quit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months after. And then I find a man, who-- an old carpenter, [? Gerson, ?] and he was working in the [NON-ENGLISH] shops. And he was-- he was making an addition, and he hired me. Instead of making $34 a week at that time in 1949, I was making $60 clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a lot of money at that time for 4 and 1/2 days because he was a Sabbath Jew. He didn't work Fridays. And I worked with him for three months till the job was finished. And at that time, I met some guys. I met George Weiner, who was the first Jew which I met in Chelsea on the square. And then he introduced me to a lot of more, more guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to work every day and come out on the square and talk to people. And they make fun of your English. And everybody asked me, how do you like this country? And I say, good. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found a job afterwards with a Boston office, partition company. $80 a week. At that time was a lot of money. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Korean War started. And the guys in this Korea say, look, you're not 26. You're only 25. You have to register for the draft. And like a schlemiel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a citizen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. So after thinking, they said, well, if they catch you, you're not registered, they may send you back to Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know, and I said, OK, myself. And I went down and I registered. And a week later, I got a letter from President Truman saying that he's very glad that I registered.

speaker: interviewee
text: To meet you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he invited me to join the Army. I know that I went to Revere at that time to register. They gave us the briefing. And then they-- they took us to South Boston. And I got up, and I said to one of the sergeants, I said, I can't speak too good English, and I am not a citizen. And he told me, sit down. And that's it. And I was in the Army. I took basic training in-- no, they took us to Fort Davis, and from Fort Davis they took us to New Jersey, Fort Dix. And after three months, they sent me to Fort Hood, Texas. That's where I took my basic training, with-- the same time I took-- Eddie Fisher was in my outfit. He took basic training with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The singer, he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Singer, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A well-known singer, Eddie Fisher.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the basic training, they assigned the 3rd Armored Division to go to Europe. And I, I didn't want to go to Europe. So I went in to the captain. And while I was in DP camp, I had a-- in, in a conc-- in the ghetto, I had a fight, and a German corroborator kicked me in my groin. And since then, I had very bad pains. And while I was in the Army, I told them that I can't walk because I was in pain always. So I always was in the hospital, see if I can get the hell out of the Army. But they didn't. And they made me as a company carpenter for the hospital, attached to the hospital in Fort Hood, Texas. And they made me a corporal. And when-- and then they made me a sergeant. And I was in charge of the whole shop in there after basic training. And I get out from going to, to Europe because I didn't want to go to Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an argument with the colonel who was in charge in the shop. He told me to make something, and I thought that this wouldn't be good. It'll be-- so the colonel went down to the captain. They put me down to go to Korea. And I came up to the, the company commander down at Fort Hood. And I said, Captain Boyce, why do you send me? I have nine-- nine and a half months to go, and I heard that if a soldier has nine months, you can't send him overseas, because it doesn't pay for the Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, look, soldier. Or it's my ass or your ass. And I was on a train to Seattle within a week. And I went to Korea. And they sent me to the-- they took away my medical MOS. Every soldier's got an MOS at training. And I-- they put me on a pipeline. That means they can send you anywhere they want. They sent me to the front line within a week when I was down there. And there was a lot of killed people there. I was 19-- there was the top of the battle in Korea. I was in Pusan, Inchon. So the commander, the captain from the hospital needed-- they had a lot of-- they needed a lot of blood, plasma. And they didn't have a lot of refrigeration. And there was a book to-- how to make refrigerations. And the, the, the colonel of the outfit looked up my MOS, and I was a carpenter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me over down here. And he said, could you do this and this? And I said, sure. Said, you're not in the front? I said, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made him two bunkers, which they worked very well. Saved a lot of-- and after I did the bunkers, they made me the company PX man. And I had two guards and whatever. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Bless you. And since then, I went on-- they gave me a, a furlough. Went to Seoul, Korea. And I find out-- I saw a Jewish star on a-- on a synagogue. It wasn't a synagogue. It was something else. And we had some-- I had a, a week for R and R. And I came back, and they sent me back to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't a citizen. They promised me to make me a citizen in the Army in Japan, which they didn't. And when I came back to Boston, they made me a citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the Army honorable discharge papers, which helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an honorable discharge, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your lovely wife? Tell us her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Used to go to dances, Jewish dances in Boston. And I-- we went to a neighborhood dance in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] north station. And I met her there dancing. I was a good dancer at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Irma Glimcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irma what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Glimcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: G-L-I-L--

speaker: interviewer
text: Glimcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: G-L-I-M-C-H-E-R. Glimcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Glimcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we went together about a year, till I proposed to her. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you working at that time, Saul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met a guy, Siegel. His name is Mr. Siegel, from Dorchester. He used to buy the old, three-story brick buildings and remodel and make into six apartments. And in 1953, when I came out from the Army, I used to make $120 clear. He used to pay me cash but-- because he didn't want to pay insurance. And I worked there for a year. And then I went into my own business with, with, with my brother. And then we broke up because he had his own family. I had my family. And since then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a family by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And since then I was working for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you and Irma have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had-- I wanted to adopt-- I almost adopted by a first marriage, Marilyn, her child. But she was at that time-- before the adoption, she was 11 years old. And the last day she decided not to be adopted. And then we have three of our own. Leann came first, then was Barry, and then was Paul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have-- Leann's got four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. What is her husband's name? It

speaker: interviewee
text: Is [? Ushanq ?] Shamash.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ushanq ?] Shamash.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ushanq ?] Shamash. He is-- he's from Iran. They, they fled right after-- right before Khomeini took over Iran. The family fled--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish family.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Israel. To Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he came down to Boston about three years before. He went to Boston to high school, and then he went to the-- to Polytech in Worcester. He is an electrical engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they have how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four. Zohar, who is a 16-year-old girl now, Eytan, who is 14, Aviv, who is 11, and Orry, who is five.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next-- your next child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My next child is Barry. He is-- he met a girl from Colombia, South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: All over the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She went to Clark University, where my-- my Barry went. And he met her there, and they married there. And he's living in Colombia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's got three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Elliot and Elliot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll think of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'll think of it. Gabriel. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Orry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Orry is my daughter's devil. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the third, your third child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Paul. Paul is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] right now in business for himself. He's got one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife's name is-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jesus Christ.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's got a kid by the name of Brett, who's two years old. Jesus Christ, my brain is not working rightly, huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's nerve wracking, bringing it down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laurie Gershkowitz. Laurie Gershkowitz Brooks. Her name is Laurie Brooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Paul's-- this is Paul's wife, my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about that grandchild in South America? You remember the third one? I have it written.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Atta boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That gonif. That gonif Daniel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to tell these grandchildren, Saul? What do you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to tell the grandchildren, I hope that they don't have to go through which I had to go through. And to be strong as a Jew. And never deny Jewish heritage. Because no matter how much we deny, Hitler used to kill the, the third and fourth generations Jews. So you don't run away from being a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain to them why this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would know to explain to them why this happened, then I would be in the history books. I would like to ask you people, why did it happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's see. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think we want to save some tape, Saul. So you want to show the photographs of your beautiful family? And is there anything that you want to add? It's all yours if you want to tell us anything.

words.text: if you want to tell us anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what can I add? I thank God I'm alive, that I brought up a nice family, nice people. And I am surrounded with nice people in Boston and in Florida. And I hope that that will help somebody to keep the Jewish heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep the Jewish heritage. Wonderful. Thank you very much, Saul. I appreciate it. OK. Saul, I see you sitting next to a very beautiful woman. Would you tell us who she is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Irma. And she has been a very good influence on me. And I hope to live longer to have more companion with her. What else can I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not much. That says it all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You bring tears to my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Now we'll see pictures of the children. Look what you do to me. OK? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father standing up. To the right is my mother, my mother. Her name is Leah Gershkowitz. My father was Boruch Gershkowitz. To the left is my aunt, Itke. I don't remember the second name. And that's her daughter, my cousin, Rachel. To the right is my sister Rachel, and my brother George, who died six years ago. And my brother Julius is sitting down there, who's alive. My sister Hannah, who died in the-- in, in the Holocaust. And this is me, the little one, which it's, I think, maybe a year and a half or two years old. And the place was, was done in Berezne, Poland. And it was in 1925 or '26. This is my father, Boruch Gershkowitz. And this is made in Berezne, Poland. And this is made in the year-- maybe right after the wedding in 1900. This is my mother, Leah. Her maiden name was Knut. And this is made in Berezne, Poland, the wedding of theirs. This is my son Barry Gershkowitz, and he's holding Gabriel in his hands. Daniel sit next to my, my son Barry, and then comes Elliot and then his wife, his wife, Berta Gershkowitz. And the photo was taken in, in Boston. The date was 1995. This is my son-in-law, [? Ushanq ?] Shamash. Next to him is the bar mitzvah boy, Eytan Shamash. Next to him is Aviv Shamash. Next to there is my daughter Leann Shamash. And Orry. And then comes my beautiful granddaughter Zohar Shamash. And this was taken in Boston in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Paul with his wife and son, Brett. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Is-- the picture was, was taken in Boston in November 1995. This is my whole family taken on my 40th anniversary with my wife at Boston in November 1995.
